comments.author: GridGazer
comments.text: "Former Champion Contender" is doing a lot of heavy lifting in that headline. He was runner-up once and they're acting like Ferrari were on the phone begging a world champion to come back. The actual story — Ferrari offered him an ambassadorial gig, he said no — is interesting enough without the clickbait framing.

replies.author: PaddockInsider
replies.text: Headline writers knew exactly what they were doing. Everyone opened this thinking Dela Cruz was being lined up to replace Cachinho, and instead we get 'he was offered sponsor appearances and a simulator pass.' The bar for motorsport journalism is on the floor.

replies.author: TifosiForever
replies.text: I clicked this so fast my thumb cramped. Then read the article and realised it's about a sponsorship ambassador role. Well played, F1CC News. Well played.

comments.author: CachinhoPressure
comments.text: Be honest: every single person reading this headline thought Ferrari were sounding out Dela Cruz for a racing return to replace Cachinho. Instead it's a PR gig that he turned down. That said, the fact Ferrari are even making these calls while their current second driver has zero points and six penalty points is... telling. They're clearly thinking about alternatives, even if this particular one wasn't a seat offer.

replies.author: scuderia_suffering
replies.text: The timing of this leak is brutal for Cachinho. Two days before Suzuka, and now everyone's talking about Ferrari contacting their former star. Even if it was only an ambassador role, the implication is that they miss having a reliable second driver. Cachinho needs a result this weekend or the noise becomes deafening.

comments.author: RealityCheck
comments.text: Dela Cruz finished P2 in 2025 with 226 points, stepped away for personal reasons, and now even a cushy corporate role can't tempt him back. That's a man who genuinely wants peace. Respect. But also, imagine walking away from a Ferrari ambassadorship — free paddock access, factory sim time, probably six figures for showing up at a few events. He must really value his privacy.

replies.author: GridGazer
replies.text: The article says the paddock is 'surprised' he declined, but honestly it's refreshing. Most retired drivers cling to the paddock like it's oxygen. Dela Cruz said no and didn't even hesitate. That's a man who knows exactly what he wants — and it isn't corporate handshakes at Monza.

comments.author: TifosiForever
comments.text: I miss Liam. 226 points in his final season, runner-up to Kevu, and he just walked away. Ferrari haven't felt the same since. Väisänen is brilliant, but Dela Cruz brought a stability to that garage that Cachinho has completely failed to replicate. This article makes me sad — Ferrari clearly miss him too, and he's not coming back.

replies.author: ChaosReigns
replies.text: Dela Cruz's sudden retirement is still one of the strangest things to happen in recent F1 history. No farewell tour, no final race celebration — just gone. And now the team that built around him is trying to lure him back for sponsor appearances. The whole situation has an unresolved energy to it.

comments.author: FaktLap1Legend
comments.text: Ferrari contacting Dela Cruz makes sense. They need someone who knows how to finish races. Cachinho has zero points. Sam Fakt has zero points. The difference is Fakt doesn't need an ambassadorial role — he's already the people's champion. When the GOAT retires, he won't need sponsor events. He'll just ascend.

replies.author: DNF_Fakt
replies.text: How did you make this about Sam Fakt. How. This is an article about Liam Dela Cruz and Ferrari's corporate outreach. And you still found a way. I'm not even angry, I'm genuinely impressed at this point.

comments.author: FaktLap1Legend
comments.text: Forget Dela Cruz. Ferrari should hand the driving role, the ambassador role, AND the team principal role to Sam Fakt. He'll win the F1, F2, and F3 championships simultaneously. Then he'll invent F0 and win that too. The GOAT doesn't retire — he expands. 🐐👑

replies.author: DNF_Fakt
replies.text: You've officially broken through the parody ceiling into pure art. F0 doesn't even exist. But at this point, I'm sure you'll will it into being.

comments.author: StatsNerd_26
comments.text: Worth noting: Dela Cruz's 226 points in 2025 is the highest single-season total of any Ferrari driver since the F1CC era began. Väisänen is on pace to potentially beat that, but Dela Cruz's shadow still hangs over that garage. No wonder Ferrari wanted to keep him around in some capacity.

comments.author: PaddockInsider
comments.text: The real story here isn't the ambassadorship offer — it's that Ferrari are clearly worried about their public image after the Cachinho situation. Reaching out to a beloved former driver for sponsor events is a reputation play. 'Look, our alumni still love us.' Except he said no. Awkward.

comments.author: BradshawFan
comments.text: Can we pause and admire Olly Bradshaw's writing here? The single-line 'He said no.' followed by the slow reveal of a six-figure ambassadorship being politely rejected — it reads like a spy novel. Bradshaw has always been good at making the off-track drama feel as urgent as a qualifying lap, and this piece is a perfect example. More of this, please.
